items.title: IEX Concall Updates Coal Exchange opportunities: Large demand on buy side and sell side with multiple sellers and merchant mines. Govt pushing for E-a...
items.description_html:
IEX Concall Updates
Coal Exchange opportunities:
Large demand on buy side and sell side with multiple sellers and merchant mines.
Govt pushing for E-auction coal to trade through exchanges.
In the Beginning stages, buyers will have to make arrangements to lift the coal themself. Price discovery, coal quality to be ensured.
MCX also filed for Coal exchange but only derivatives, IEX to be spot market.
TAM can be told only after final regulations.

Operations side:
More than 70% trading is through API. Price discovery is through algorithm and using AI for only creating new products and operational improvement.

Gas exchange:
Volumes not as good as expected, due to middle east crisis. Q1 we may not grow, but definitely in Q2 we will see growth. Domestic + other suppliers too trading here.
Deadline to reduce stake in IGX extended to Dec 31st 2026.

Market Coupling:

When asked for a timeline, management says “can't say when final notification will be issued”.
On the new draft about Grid India deciding price, management says “situation is fluid”.
“They might review this decision or may not go ahead at all.”
“All exchanges have their own software. Grid India has to create infrastructure, software and connect all these exchanges. Bids will be collected and submitted to Grid India. I don't think so any value creation is happening.
No benefit of coupling”
As for trading participant’s response to coupling, no change in their working. Customer loyalty is very important. We interact with them, teach them how to use software, data analytics, faster bidding, settlement support, so a lot of value addition from IEX. I am sure we will retain our customers. Even if coupling happens, we will retain our market share. Liquidity will continue. We don't expect any significant margin drop.
RTM no case of market coupling anywhere. Tight timeline 15 minute and 5 minutes time block. Not possible to implement.
Renewable suppliers and battery suppliers number increasing everyday.

FY27 volume growth will be maintained at 15-20% growth. This year will have high demand.

CFD contracts pilot launch for 500MW fixed price battery capacity.

items.title: CDMO is a difficult domain to understand and explain. It’s full of nuances. The 2020 IIC presentation was much easier for me. That was open and acces...
items.description_html:
CDMO is a difficult domain to understand and explain. It’s full of nuances. The 2020 IIC presentation was much easier for me. That was open and accessible.

When I looked for professional help on the CDMO book, I found that most editors lacked domain knowledge. The choice became clear: either dilute the technical depth or keep the nuance and accept the complexity. I was reminded that some things can be made simple, but not simpler.

After writing a technically strong, if somewhat dry, first book, this next topic feels more natural. It’s rooted in my own journey, making it easier to write and discuss with an editor for proofreading.

The lessons from Book 1 (framing business uncertainty) carry forward here, or at least that is the goal. This time, the format is simpler. A shorter book. A lower price. A much broader context.

In the end, the reader will decide.

items.description_html:
Infosys closes near a 5 year low

Valuations have compressed sharply, multiples now close to ~15x among the lowest levels seen in a decade

Generates ~$4 Bn OCF & sitting on ~$4.5 Bn cash

Message from the market is quite clear - dividends & buybacks are no longer enough
Similar trends are visible across peers as well

Unless they re-invent themselves in the AI led services cycle, the next few years are likely to remain structurally challenging for the sector

items.title: .@_HannahRitchie, our Deputy Editor and Science Outreach Lead, has won the 2026 Unwin Award! The award recognizes “non-fiction writers in the earlier...
items.description_html:
.@_HannahRitchie, our Deputy Editor and Science Outreach Lead, has won the 2026 Unwin Award!

The award recognizes “non-fiction writers in the earlier stages of their careers as authors, whose work is considered to have made a significant contribution to the world.”

It’s awarded for an author’s overall body of work. Hannah has written two books:

– Clearing the Air: A Hopeful Guide to Solving Climate Change in 50 Questions and Answers
– Not the End of the World: Surprising facts, dangerous myths and hopeful solutions for our future on planet Earth

The award’s judging panel praised "Not the End of the World" as “a well-written and revealing book and for its optimistic and data-grounded approach which gives readers hope for the future of the planet.”

The award comes with a £10,000 prize, which Hannah decided to donate to the Against Malaria Foundation.

Congratulations, Hannah!

Read more in the award announcement: https://www.publishers.org.uk/hannah-ritchie-wins-unwin-award-2026/

items.title: RT Sridhar Vembu: I studied the life and work of Lee Kuan Yew, the Founder-Prime Minister of Singapore intensely during my early 20s. Immense respect ...
items.description_html:
RT Sridhar Vembu
I studied the life and work of Lee Kuan Yew, the Founder-Prime Minister of Singapore intensely during my early 20s. Immense respect for him.

In this video clip, he advises a young woman pursuing a PhD not to neglect marriage and kids. I recently gave very similar advice.

Elon Musk would say much the same thing.

We have to orient our culture, society and the economy so that smart ambitious women do want to have children (and sadly, this needs to be said today: men cannot have children). If we fail at this, humanity will lose.

Now let the attacks begin! 🤓


Alvin Foo: PHD is good but please don’t waste time… - Lee Kuan Yew 😂

items.title: IKS Health is acquiring 100% of TruBridge Transaction is expected to be EPS accretive from the first full year itself Management targeting ~3000 Cr EB...
items.description_html:
IKS Health is acquiring 100% of TruBridge

Transaction is expected to be EPS accretive from the first full year itself

Management targeting ~3000 Cr EBITDA for the combined entity by FY30 (vs 1000 Cr currently)

TruBridge brings a proprietary Electronic Health Record (EHR) platform which acts as the system of record

IKS has identified a ~$575 Mn whitespace opportunity within TruBridge’s existing client base which can be tapped through cross selling RCM services, documentation tools & platform solutions

Management expects cost synergies through global delivery optimization & efficiencies which should support EBITDA improvement as integration progresses over the next few years

Post acquisition leverage is expected to be around ~3x
Management has demonstrated ability to deleverage earlier after the Aquity acquisition within ~2 years

Overall this acquisition strengthens platform capability, expands market reach, improves cross sell opportunities & moves IKS structurally closer towards becoming an integrated healthcare technology platform rather than a pure RCM services player

items.title: Literacy - the ability to read and write a sentence in any language with proper comprehension - is the most fundamental measure of educational outcome...
items.description_html:
Literacy - the ability to read and write a sentence in any language with proper comprehension - is the most fundamental measure of educational outcomes.

India has made remarkable progress in improving literacy rates, rising from fewer than two in ten literate in the early 1950s, to eight in ten as of 2023. India’s literacy data comes from the Census (until 2011) and subsequent nationally representative sample surveys by the National Statistics Office (NSO).

Literacy in India lags behind the world average, but the data shows that that is largely a historical legacy. Youth literacy (ages 15-24) is rising fast and is now higher than the world average.

Half of India's illiterate population is above 50 years of age. Meanwhile, illiteracy has been virtually eliminated among children and teenagers suggesting that India is on the path to near-universal literacy.

Despite this progress in literacy, gaps remain. Not all groups are at equal levels of literacy - urban literacy is higher than rural, and literacy is higher for men than women in India. Illiteracy is also higher among people who belong to a marginalised group. But these gaps have narrowed over time as well.

What are the literacy rates across various states in India? Read @akwaghmare's piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/literacy-in-india/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Literacy #Education #LivingConditions #India #DataForIndia

Kotak Inst Eqt on Union Bank
Buy, TP Rs 210
Union Bank’s earnings grew 7% yoy, led by ~30% yoy decline in provisions, while PPOP grew 3% yoy. RoA stood at 1.4% and RoE at 17%.

Expect NIMs to witness stable to slightly improving trends, supported by a continued repricing of deposits

Gold and Silver under pressure

Gold prices ‌fell to a more than one-week low on Thursday, pressured by worries that inflationary fallout from the Middle East conflict could keep interest rates higher for longer.

* *Spot gold* was down 0.9% at $4,697.06 per ounce, as of 03:05 p.m. EDT (1905 GMT), after hitting its lowest level since April 13. Bullion fell more than 1% earlier in the day to as ⁠low as $4,663.69 per ounce.
* *U.S. gold futures* for June delivery settled 0.6% lower to $4,724.
* *Spot silver* fell 2.7% to $75.55 per ounce.
* *Platinum* lost 3.2% to $2,008.22, both having hit more than one-week lows earlier. *Palladium* was down 5% at $1,465.23.

items.title: Shocking stat of the day: AI-related stocks now reflect a record 45% of the S&P 500's market cap. This percentage has surged +20 points since November...
items.description_html:
Shocking stat of the day:

AI-related stocks now reflect a record 45% of the S&P 500's market cap.

This percentage has surged +20 points since November 2022, when ChatGPT was launched.

Furthermore, a record 15.4% of investment-grade debt is now tied to AI, making it the largest sector in the US credit market.

This percentage has risen +3.5 points since 2020.

This comes as AI-linked debt has nearly DOUBLED over this period to an all-time high of $1.4 trillion.

Never before has a single theme dominated both US equity and credit markets to this magnitude.

items.title: RT Gaurab Chakrabarti: Stella Chemifa started making fluoride compounds for toothpaste. Today they produce hydrofluoric acid at 12N purity: 99.9999999...
items.description_html:
RT Gaurab Chakrabarti
Stella Chemifa started making fluoride compounds for toothpaste. Today they produce hydrofluoric acid at 12N purity: 99.9999999999%. TSMC, Samsung, Intel, and SK Hynix depend on it to etch every advanced chip on earth. In July 2019, Japan restricted hydrogen fluoride exports to South Korea. Shipments dropped 87.9% in a month. Korea produces over half the world's memory chips, and had no alternative supplier at that purity. South Korea poured billions into domestic production. For the most advanced nodes, they still import from Japan. Getting from five nines to twelve nines took Stella Chemifa decades of process refinement. Every extra nine means ten times fewer contaminants. The global chip supply chain runs through 723 people in Osaka operating on toothpaste chemistry.

items.title: China is rerouting its chip equipment imports amid US export controls: China's semiconductor equipment imports from Singapore rose +17% YoY in 2025, t...
items.description_html:
China is rerouting its chip equipment imports amid US export controls:

China's semiconductor equipment imports from Singapore rose +17% YoY in 2025, to a record $5.7 billion.

At the same time, imports from Malaysia surged over +100% YoY, to $3.4 billion, also an all-time high.

Meanwhile, chip tool imports from the US plunged -34% YoY, to ~$2.0 billion, the lowest since 2017.

The Netherlands and Japan remain China's largest sources of chip equipment by origin, at ~$9.9 billion and ~$8.5 billion, respectively.

Over the 2020-2025 period, China's cumulative chip tool imports from Japan totaled over $42.0 billion, followed by the Netherlands at $35.0 billion.

US semiconductor export controls are redefining the market.

items.title: RT Jakub Wiech: Mili Państwo, mogło Wam to umknąć, ale: w 2025 r. źródła odnawialne przebiły węgiel w światowej produkcji energii elektryczn...
items.description_html:
RT Jakub Wiech
Mili Państwo, mogło Wam to umknąć, ale: w 2025 r. źródła odnawialne przebiły węgiel w światowej produkcji energii elektrycznej.

🌍Jak podaje Ember, stało się tak po raz pierwszy od 100 lat, ale w zupełnie innej skali niż wiek temu. W zeszłym roku OZE wygenerowały łącznie 10 730 TWh (33,8% światowej produkcji prądu), a węgiel 10 476 TWh (33%).

Za 1/3 globalnego miksu generacyjnego odpowiadają już zatem źródła odnawialne.

Ale to nie wszystko. Spójrzcie na resztę najnowszych danych:

🔥Udział węgla w globalnej produkcji prądu spadł do najniższego poziomu w historii;

☀️W 2025 roku aż 75% światowego wzrostu zapotrzebowania na energię elektryczną pokryło jedno źródło - fotowoltaika. Pozostałą część pokryły inne źródła bezemisyjne;

📈Ubiegłoroczny wzrost produkcji energii elektryczności z samych tylko paneli słonecznych wyniósł 636 TWh - to mniej więcej 4 razy tyle, ile rocznie zużywa Polska. Z kolei całość globalnej produkcji z PV sięgnęła 2778 TWh, co oznacza wzrost o 100% od 2022 roku i o 900% w porównaniu do roku 2015;

📉Gwałtowny rozwój OZE sprawił, że w ubiegłym roku produkcja prądu z paliw kopalnych spadła rokrocznie o 0,2%. To pierwszy taki spadek od pandemii.

Jak się zsumuje OZE i atom to wychodzi, że wkrótce połowa globalnej produkcji prądu będzie już bezemisyjna. To jest nasz wielki sukces.

items.title: Traders are positioning for a major shift in US funding markets: Daily trading volume in futures tied to the spread between the Secured Overnight Fina...
items.description_html:
Traders are positioning for a major shift in US funding markets:

Daily trading volume in futures tied to the spread between the Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR) and the Federal Funds Rate surged to a record 56,590 contracts on Tuesday.

SOFR is the cost of borrowing cash overnight against US Treasury securities, and is closely tied to the Fed interest rate, making it one of the most important reference rates in global financial markets.

Currently, SOFR is trading at 3.63%, just below the federal funds rate of 3.64%, a sign that cash is still plentiful in the financial system.

Traders are now betting this spread will turn positive, expecting borrowing costs to rise above the Fed's rate, signaling that cash is becoming harder to access.

This comes as the Fed is cutting its monthly Treasury bill purchases to $25 billion from $40 billion, while the Treasury is expected to increase the size of its T-bill auctions in the coming weeks to rebuild its cash balance toward a $900 billion target by the end of June.

Both moves reduce the amount of cash available in the financial system, tightening liquidity conditions that have kept overnight borrowing cheap throughout 2026.

Money market liquidity is set to shrink.

items.description_html:
Institutional investors are rebuilding their equity exposure:

The NAAIM Exposure Index has jumped +19 points over the last 4 weeks, to 79, the highest since mid-February.

This index measures average US equity exposure reported by members of the National Association of Active Investment Managers, making it a key indicator of professional investor positioning.

At the same time, hedge fund net leverage has risen +8 percentage points to 77%, the highest since early March.

This metric shows the amount that hedge funds are betting on the market going up versus down, with higher readings indicating more aggressive positioning.

Both metrics remain well below their December 2025 peaks, suggesting the positioning rebuild is not yet complete.

Institutional equity exposure is quickly recovering.

Crude oil and petroleum product exports have DOUBLED since January 2022.

This comes as Asian and European nations scramble to secure alternative supply following the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Meanwhile, US crude oil inventories excluding the Strategic Petroleum Reserve rose +1.9 million barrels, to 465.7 million barrels, the highest since 2023.

World demand for American oil is skyrocketing.

items.description_html:
Without even reading the article, I agree!

There are times in Bombay, when you can’t hear yourself think! The best part of getting out of the big city (ies)—- the lower decibels of noise!


Kush Katakia: There is an article in this week's @TheEconomist. It features a video with sound of a busy street in London, alongside a video of a street in Delhi. You have to experience the article and the difference in noise levels. It is absolutely brilliant.

items.title: PE is important, but you need to see it along with growth as PE can be misleading – Ramesh Damani Sir's case study – "If you see all my picks - Info...
items.description_html:
PE is important, but you need to see it along with growth as PE can be misleading –

Ramesh Damani Sir's case study –

"If you see all my picks - Infosys, CMC, McDowells, Balmer Lawrie, Gruh Finance - the first filter was the market cap - how cheap is the company in relation to the size of the opportunity or where it could be after 10 years. I was not even concerned about the earnings.

One of the companies I bought was E-Serve - the BPO arm of Citibank. We bought it at a market cap of Rs. 150 Cr, when it was earning a profit of Rs. 1 Cr, so it was at 150 P/E. Since this was after Infosys, I was asked quite often those days why I was buying E-Serve. The reason I bought it was that I was convinced that BPO would be a big business after technology, and it would generate so much employment.

I think McKinsey was forecasting a USD 10-15 billion industry in five years, and it made intuitive sense to me. I knew how computers worked and that you could do work at a much lower price in India. I knew that the leader could have a 10% market share, and one should buy the leader.

E-serve happened to be listed and was growing at a 30-40% CAGR. We made tons of money. I didn’t care about the earnings or P/E. You can’t look at earnings in isolation, as the market cap was puny.

Though we were kind of cheated out of the position, as it was delisted at a market cap, which was equal to the next two years of profits. But there was no option. We made 6x in three years. People who did not tender shares got shares of TCS. I had not tendered, so I got TCS shares, which went up another 5x. So we did fairly decently. This also marked my transition as an investor. So far, I had bought cheap companies, now I had bought a growth company at 150 P/E."

From the Book Masterclass with Super Investors; no stock reco

Side Note - This is how we pick stocks too, opportunity size is under rated, PE is over rated sometimes!

items.title: RT Aditya Kalra: ⚖️ 🍔 India's Zomato drops a price parity clause from its contracts after a revolt by restaurants who said it undermined their of...
items.description_html:
RT Aditya Kalra
⚖️ 🍔 India's Zomato drops a price parity clause from its contracts after a revolt by restaurants who said it undermined their offline pricing decisions.

⚖️Zomato was empowered earlier to use mystery shopping to detect any lower-than-app prices, according to Reuters review of these contracts

⚖️Five Lawyers and a former Indian antitrust official who reviewed the clause for @Reuters flagged it as anti-competitive.

⚖️What did the clause say? "Zomato may use various means including but not limited to customer complaints and mystery shopping to identify price disparity between a Restaurant's offline (dine-in) menu prices, and the prices on the Platform. The Restaurant Partner agrees that this amount will be deducted from its ongoing payouts." And it explained "Price Disparity": "The Restaurant Partner will at all times maintain equal or lower prices for all its products offered to the Customers via the Platform as against the prices through its direct channels"

READ. https://www.reuters.com/world/india/indian-food-delivery-giant-zomato-drops-pricing-clause-after-pushback-source-2026-04-23/

items.title: Tata Capital delivered a mixed Q4 FY26 with strong top-line loan growth of 20.8% and margin expansion beating cost-to-income guidance, but missed ROA/...
items.description_html:
Tata Capital delivered a mixed Q4 FY26 with strong top-line loan growth of 20.8% and margin expansion beating cost-to-income guidance, but missed ROA/ROE/PAT targets.

The financing segment margin inflections to 25.1% and improved capital adequacy to 18.96% are positives, while asset quality deterioration (GNPA 2.61%, NNPA 1.15%) remains a concern. Execution is good with operating leverage visible, but profitability guidance misses temper enthusiasm.

Positive Factors

Loan book grew 20.8% YoY to ₹2,68,203 Cr, meeting upper end of 18-20% guidance

NII margin expanded to 46.4% from 43.0% YoY, indicating margin expansion

Cost-to-income ratio improved to 34.8% from 39.4% YoY, beating 38-39% guidance by 320-420 bps

Financing segment margin expanded to 25.1% from 21.4% YoY, a 370 bps improvement

PPOP grew 21.0% YoY to ₹9,585 Cr, demonstrating strong operating leverage

CAR improved to 18.96% from 16.80% YoY, equity grew 37.6% to ₹47,107 Cr

PAT grew 32.2% YoY to ₹4,846 Cr with normalized growth of 33.1%

Risk Factors

ROA of 1.80% missed guidance of 2.0-2.1% by 20-30 bps

ROE of 12.28% missed guidance of 13-14% by 72-172 bps

PAT growth of 32.2% missed ~35% guidance target

GNPA deteriorated to 2.61% from 2.33% YoY, up 28 bps

NNPA at 1.15% missed <1.0% guidance, up 17 bps YoY

Investment Activity segment swung to loss of ₹95.88
Cr in Q4 from profit in Q3

items.title: Adani Energy Solutions reported mixed Q4 FY26 results with strong 16.8% revenue growth driven by Smart Meter segment (582% YoY), but profitability dec...
items.description_html:
Adani Energy Solutions reported mixed Q4 FY26 results with strong 16.8% revenue growth driven by Smart Meter segment (582% YoY), but profitability declined as expenses grew faster (24.9% YoY). The Smart Meter business is emerging as a new growth engine with 18x EBIT growth, achieving the 1 crore meter target ahead of schedule.

However, margin compression of 240 bps and missed capex guidance (₹14,482 Cr vs ₹16,000-18,000 Cr guided) indicate execution challenges. The company maintains strong cash conversion (4.6x PAT) but is in a heavy capex cycle with negative free cash flow.

Positive Factors

Smart Meter segment revenue grew 582% YoY and EBIT grew 1457% YoY, becoming new growth driver

Revenue grew 16.8% YoY to ₹7,443 Cr with all business segments contributing

Achieved 1 crore cumulative smart meters target ahead of schedule

Transmission segment EBIT grew 9.4% YoY with robust 35.5% margin

Operating cash flow at 4.6x PAT indicates high-quality earnings

System availability maintained at 99.77% in Q4 FY26

Distribution losses improved to 4.20% from 4.31% YoY

Risk Factors

Total expenses grew 24.9% YoY outpacing revenue growth of 16.8%

EBITDA margin compressed 240 bps YoY to 29.9%

PBT declined 6.6% YoY to ₹910 Cr due to cost pressure

FY26 capex of ₹14,482 Cr missed guidance of ₹16,000-18,000 Cr by 9%

Other expenses increased sharply by 81.9% YoY

Debt-equity ratio worsened to 1.84x from 1.73x

Current ratio deteriorated to 1.15x from 1.46x

items.title: Media and most market participants are trapped by short horizons. They judge a long-duration business with a trader’s stopwatch. Torrent and JB may h...
items.description_html:
Media and most market participants are trapped by short horizons. They judge a long-duration business with a trader’s stopwatch.
Torrent and JB may have lagged over brief windows. So what. In pharma, the relevant clock is 5 years, better 10. Anything shorter is mostly noise dressed up as insight.
Skill is never pure; luck is always in the room. But time is the filter. Extend the horizon and luck washes out enough for skill to compound, and occasionally reveal itself violently on the upside.

Play long term games with clear long term goals and only with long term management.

items.description_html:
HSCL delivered strong FY26 with PAT growth of 35.2% and margin expansion of 292 bps, meeting its margin sustainability target.

The new capacity (70,000 MTPA speciality carbon black line) and acquisitions (Birla Tyres) provide near-term growth catalysts, though volume impact was not visible in Q4. Cash flow deterioration due to working capital and capex is a near-term concern but aligns with the planned investment cycle for future growth.

Positive Factors

PAT grew 35.2% YoY to ₹751.34 Cr in FY26, on track for FY28 doubling goal

EBITDA margin expanded 292 bps YoY to 20.4% driven by operational efficiency and high value-added products

FY26 capex of ₹636.64 Cr executed within guided ₹600-700 Cr range

New Others segment (acquisitions) scaled rapidly to ₹157.92 Cr revenue in Q4 with 16.1% EBIT margin

Core Carbon materials EBIT margin expanded 374 bps to 19.0% despite flat revenue

Company migrated to lower tax regime (Sec 115BAA) reducing ETR from 31.1% to 24.6%

Risk Factors

Operating cash flow declined 14.5% YoY to ₹382 Cr due to ₹370 Cr working capital outflow

Free cash flow negative at ₹254.64 Cr as company is in heavy investment cycle

Net cash position reduced from ₹367.28 Cr to ₹93.68 Cr with borrowings doubling to ₹766.56 Cr

Core Carbon materials segment revenue down 0.4% YoY in Q4, volume growth not yet visible

Forex loss of ₹38.13 Cr in Q4 FY26 vs gain in prior year

items.title: Khaitan Chemicals reported strong FY26 full-year performance with 39.1% revenue growth and PBT turning positive from losses. However, Q4 showed a shar...
items.description_html:
Khaitan Chemicals reported strong FY26 full-year performance with 39.1% revenue growth and PBT turning positive from losses. However, Q4 showed a sharp sequential decline with 27.4% QoQ revenue drop and 37.1% YoY PAT decline.

The Fertilizers segment turnaround is confirmed with 591 bps margin expansion, but the Chemicals segment faces margin pressure. Balance sheet strengthened with Debt/Equity improving to 1.04x, though cash conversion remains weak with CFO/PAT at 0.64x due to working capital blockage. No management guidance was provided for comparison.

Positive Factors

FY26 revenue grew 39.1% YoY to ₹1,00,162.94 Lakhs

Fertilizers segment EBIT grew 406.2% YoY with margin expansion of 591 bps

Debt reduced by 3.7% YoY, Debt/Equity improved from 1.40x to 1.04x

Equity base expanded 30.5% YoY to ₹29,064.88 Lakhs

Free cash flow positive at ₹3,531.28 Lakhs for FY26

PBT swung to profit of ₹7,172.75 Lakhs in FY26 from loss of ₹1,649.98 Lakhs in FY25

Risk Factors

Q4 PAT from continuing operations declined 37.1% YoY to ₹542.39 Lakhs

Revenue declined 27.4% QoQ in Q4 showing sharp momentum loss

items.description_html:
RT StockScans By SOIC
We receive a lot of emails asking what to focus on in Market Scans.

If your goal is to spot clear leaders without confusion, just track industries with the tag “Outperforming” these are already showing strong relative strength and leadership.

But if you want to go one step ahead and catch early moves, then focus on the score. Industries with a rising Score Change 1M are often the ones recovering from a base or transitioning out of Stage 4, showing improving momentum.

If you check MarketScans now it is showing industries like -
1. Electrodes - Welding Equipment
2. Building Material USA
3. Water Treatment
4. Steel - Tubes/Pipes.
To check the industry constituents to check it out here: https://www.stockscans.in/market-scans

Disc: Not a Buy/Sell Recommendations.

items.title: ABSLAMC reported mixed Q4 results with 6.85% YoY revenue growth but 17.96% PAT decline due to MTM losses in Other Income. While EBITDA margin expanded...
items.description_html:
ABSLAMC reported mixed Q4 results with 6.85% YoY revenue growth but 17.96% PAT decline due to MTM losses in Other Income. While EBITDA margin expanded 126 bps to 58.14% demonstrating operating leverage, the full-year revenue missed guidance (9.5% vs 10-12% target).

The company maintains strong cash generation (0.83x CFO/PAT) and recommended healthy dividends, but faces headwinds from market share decline in SIP flows and recent talent departures in key segments.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 6.85% YoY to ₹458.23 crore in Q4

EBITDA grew 9.22% YoY to ₹266.42 crore with 126 bps margin expansion

Operating PBT (normalized) grew 8.2% YoY excluding MTM losses

CFO to PAT conversion strong at 0.83x with FCF of ₹764.44 crore

Net worth grew 8.4% YoY to ₹4,041.66 crore

Recommended dividend of ₹25.50 per share

Risk Factors

Revenue missed FY26 guidance of 10-12%, delivering 9.5% growth

PAT declined 17.96% YoY due to ₹105 crore negative swing in Other Income

Other Income swung to loss of ₹32.86 crore from gain of ₹71.96 crore

PAT growth of 4.8% lagged revenue growth of 9.5% for FY26

Subsidiary operations reported net loss of ₹10.62 crore for FY26

Departure of Heads of Passives and Alternate Investments in Q4

items.title: Union Bank delivered a solid Q4 with 6.6% YoY PAT growth driven by 74.7% YoY collapse in NPA provisions and 6.9% reduction in operating expenses. Asse...
items.description_html:
Union Bank delivered a solid Q4 with 6.6% YoY PAT growth driven by 74.7% YoY collapse in NPA provisions and 6.9% reduction in operating expenses. Asset quality reached multi-year lows (GNPA 2.82%, NNPA 0.48%) with strong PCR of 95.03%. Corporate Banking segment was a standout with 43.8% PBT growth and 600bps margin expansion, successfully managing the ₹30,000 crore book churn.

While NII declined 1.1% YoY (margin pressure materialized as guided) and Treasury operations weak, ROA target of >1% was met and credit costs stayed within 20-40bps guidance. The proactive ₹700 crore provision for new ECL norms and strong capital position (CRAR 18.10%) provide balance sheet resilience.

Positive Factors

PPOP grew 3.3% YoY to ₹7,955 crore driven by 6.9% reduction in Operating Expenses

Net profit grew 6.6% YoY to ₹5,316 crore with NPA provisions down 74.7% YoY

Corporate Banking PBT surged 43.8% YoY with margins expanding from 13.7% to 19.6%

Retail Banking PBT grew 20.0% YoY with margins improving from 19.5% to 22.3%

GNPA improved to 2.82% and NNPA to 0.48%, both at multi-year lows

CRAR at 18.10% and CET1 at 15.69% provide strong capital position

One-time proactive provision of ₹700 crore

strengthens balance sheet for new ECL norms

Risk Factors

NII declined 1.1% YoY indicating ongoing margin pressure from rate environment

Treasury Operations PBT fell 34.1% YoY with revenue down 20.0% YoY

Deposit growth of 2.7% YoY lagged behind advances growth of 10.5% YoY

Full year PPOP declined 8.0% YoY impacted by NII pressure and higher opex earlier in the year

NIM target of 2.76% was not met - margin compression materialized as guided

items.title: RAJESH delivered strong FY26 results beating its own 40% CAGR guidance on revenue (51.9% YoY), EBITDA (59.7% YoY), and PAT (48.2% YoY). Margin expande...
items.description_html:
RAJESH delivered strong FY26 results beating its own 40% CAGR guidance on revenue (51.9% YoY), EBITDA (59.7% YoY), and PAT (48.2% YoY). Margin expanded 59 bps to 12.15% demonstrating operating leverage.

However, severe cash conversion issues emerged with negative CFO of ₹(4,142) Lakhs driven by working capital stress (receivables +92% YoY, inventories +99% YoY). New BESS business entry provides a future growth catalyst. The beat on guidance is confirmed but execution quality is tempered by cash flow concerns.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 51.9% YoY to ₹1,627.94 Cr, exceeding 40% CAGR guidance

PAT grew 48.2% YoY to ₹143.20 Cr, exceeding 40% CAGR guidance

EBITDA grew 59.7% YoY to ₹197.80 Cr, exceeding 40% CAGR guidance

EBITDA margin expanded 59 bps YoY to 12.15%

Finance costs declined 27.4% YoY to ₹10.90 Cr, improving profitability

H2 execution confirmed at 60.8% of annual revenue vs guided 55-60%

New BESS business driver with 65MW/130MWh project and subsidiary formed

Risk Factors

CFO deeply negative at ₹(4,142) Lakhs vs PAT of ₹14,320 Lakhs - severe cash conversion issue

Trade receivables grew 91.9% YoY vs revenue growth of 51.9%

Inventories grew 98.8% YoY, indicating working capital stress

Cash and cash equivalents fell sharply from ₹2,218 Lakhs to ₹135 Lakhs

Working capital blockage of over ₹24,400 Lakhs from receivables and inventory

Dependence on government contracts remains high with no diversification visible

items.title: RT Johan Fourie: Will AI replace us or relocate our labour ? Yesterday I gave a talk to twenty-five of Stellenbosch University’s Heads of Department ...
items.description_html:
RT Johan Fourie
Will AI replace us or relocate our labour ?

Yesterday I gave a talk to twenty-five of Stellenbosch University’s Heads of Department about artificial intelligence.

When I started preparing the talk a week ago, two of the tools I ended up using did not yet exist. Opus 4.7, the model I use most, was released only last Thursday. Claude Design, which I used to build the slides, appeared the next day. If I had given the same talk a month ago, several of my examples would not have been available. A week from now, parts of this essay will already read as slightly dated.

General-purpose technologies are an old story: the plough, steam, electricity, the computer. Each reallocated labour rather than eliminating it, but each also rewarded some people, places, and organisations sooner than others. The interesting question has never been ‘will it replace us?’ It has always been ‘where does labour go, and who gets there first?’

items.title: RT Intrinsic Compounding: Two really interesting results in Aerospace and Power theme: 1. GE Aerospace: Demand continues to be strong for commercial E...
items.description_html:
RT Intrinsic Compounding
Two really interesting results in Aerospace and Power theme:

1. GE Aerospace: Demand continues to be strong for commercial Engines

GE Aerospace (NYSE:GE) reported better-than-expected first quarter financial results, driven by sharp growth in its commercial engines and services business and a significant increase in orders.

Total orders for the quarter reached $23 billion, an 87% increase from the prior year period, supported by demand for commercial engine services, which rose 39%.

2. GE Vernova: Gas Turbines and AI fuelled power demand

Power unit's core profit rose 57% to $811 million in the first quarter, while the electrification unit's profit more than doubled to $528 million.

GE Vernova also expects higher gas power revenue in the second half of the ⁠year as it ships more turbines and ramps up capacity to nearly 20 GW

The company added $13 billion to its backlog, taking the total to $163 ‌billion. It now ⁠expects the backlog to reach $200 billion by 2027, a year earlier than its previous forecast.

Multiple proxies to these trends are available in India

items.title: RT Govindraj Ethiraj: Fuel prices not raised for 4 years. But not raising prices now with crude > $90-100 for weeks is sending wrong signals encouragi...
items.description_html:
RT Govindraj Ethiraj
Fuel prices not raised for 4 years.
But not raising prices now with crude > $90-100 for weeks is sending wrong signals encouraging consumption when curtailing is need of hour.
Most analysts are saying petrol is underpriced by Rs 18-20 and diesel by Rs 30-35.
This is the math.
Whether Govt raises prices or not is a policy call.
But not moderating consumption during an energy shock will create more challenges later.

items.title: Taiwan's stock market just made history: The total market cap of Taiwan's stock market is up to $4.14 trillion, surpassing the UK's $4.09 trillion for...
items.description_html:
Taiwan's stock market just made history:

The total market cap of Taiwan's stock market is up to $4.14 trillion, surpassing the UK's $4.09 trillion for the first time.

Taiwan's market cap has TRIPLED since 2020, driven almost entirely by the AI-fueled surge in semiconductor stocks.

Over this period, Taiwan Semiconductor, $TSM, which makes up more than 40% of Taiwan's total market value, has surged +680% and is trading near its all-time high.

By comparison, the UK's market remains roughly in-line with its 2013 peak and pre-Financial Crisis highs.

Meanwhile, foreign investors purchased +$8.9 billion of Taiwanese shares so far in April, on track for the largest monthly inflow on record.

AI is reshaping the global stock market.

items.title: Super Interesting - China runs one of the most generous R&D tax incentive regimes in the world. As of 2026, nearly 85% of government support for busin...
items.description_html:
Super Interesting -

China runs one of the most generous R&D tax incentive regimes in the world. As of 2026, nearly 85% of government support for business R&D flows through tax incentives.

Companies can claim a 200% pre-tax deduction on eligible R&D expenses.

Machine tool and semiconductor manufacturers receive an even higher 220% deduction until 2027.

Eligible costs include researcher salaries, materials, prototype design, testing, depreciation & maintenance. Businesses are, indeed, leveraging and benefiting from state-sponsored support

Every dollar a Chinese firm spends on innovation costs them less than fifty cents.

Innovation isn't an option anymore, it's a necessity!

items.title: The gap between older and younger equity holders has never been wider: Americans aged 70+ now hold a record 17% of all US equities and mutual fund sha...
items.description_html:
The gap between older and younger equity holders has never been wider:

Americans aged 70+ now hold a record 17% of all US equities and mutual fund shares outstanding.

The percentage has TRIPLED since the 2008 Financial Crisis low.

At the same time, Americans under 40 own just ~3%, in-line with 2003 levels.

This percentage is HALF of what it was in the late 1980s.

As a result, the difference between the two cohorts is now ~14 percentage points, the widest on record.

By comparison, the gap stood at ~6 percentage points at the start of the 1990s.

The wealth gap is widening.

items.title: *Havells India Ltd Q4FY26 Concall Update* (Nirmal Bang Retail Research) *#Overall weighted average price growth across categories can be estimated in ...
items.description_html:
*Havells India Ltd Q4FY26 Concall Update*
(Nirmal Bang Retail Research)

*#Overall weighted average price growth across categories can be estimated in the range of 5-10%*
*#Major capex for FY27 & FY28 is allocated to Cables & Wires (C&W) and a R&D Centre*

*Outlook: Long-term Positive*

*Key Highlights*
•Overall quarter performance was modest with just 2.4% yoy revenue growth and margins at gross / EBITDA level both declined by ~70bps yoy.
•There was an impact on cooling products inventory due to mild start to summer season. Industrial infra demand remained strong, but consumer categories experienced challenges amid inflationary pressures from global disruptions.
•Company’s A&P spends surged 24% yoy / 14% yoy for higher visibility. Also, it recognized one-time gain of Rs. 283 cr on account of its unrealized gain on the investment in Goldi Solar Pvt Ltd in Q1FY26. The same has led to 40%+ growth in its reported PAT. Adj PAT excluding one-time gain reported a decline of ~15% yoy at Rs. 440 Cr.
•Cables performed well with sustained growth in cables while wires saw a muted performance. C&W overall volume growth stood at 6% yoy. Segment margins were supported by rising copper prices; however, pricing growth was estimated at ~8% yoy, that shows company’s calibrated approach in pricing actions to maintain the market share, growth and margins.
•Lighting revenues posted a stable growth of ~2% yoy, as LED lighting prices are stabilizing. The segment contribution margin for the quarter stood ~37% which is led by a one off like year-end releases for the entire year and it expects long term average remains in the range of 30-32% at normalized level.
•ECD revenues (mainly fans and air coolers) impacted due to delayed summer and unseasonal rains.
•In others category, there was a strong scale up in renewables space. Havells is focusing on adding more solar capacity and aspire to take the advantage of tailwinds of current demand scenario. Upcoming year expects lot more opportunities to drive more penetration and gain volumes. Better product additions to support the margins while focusing on gaining share in such competitive market.
•Lloyd business moderated due to previous year strong base and delay in summers. Margins impacted due to lower revenues. It has witnessed higher inventory in the 1st half of April month due to slower sales, and likely to be normalized by the end of this month. It can expect increase in the prices in 8%-15% range.
•Switchgears margins impacted due to lag in passing of cost; expected to normalize in coming quarters.
•Cooling products like Acs, fans and air coolers have seen price increase during Q4FY26 led by implementation of new BEE norms and further increase is expected on the back of increase in RM prices in the near to medium term while maintaining balance in the market share and margins.
Stock is trading at P/E of 38x FY28E EPS


Yatin Mota: Havells results below estimates

Need to check on inventory gains.

That could have led to beat in Wire and Cables business

items.title: When Ashok Damani jokingly said to his father, Ramesh Damani, that he had met the NBCC management more than he had met his fiancée! Ramesh Damani Sir...
items.description_html:
When Ashok Damani jokingly said to his father, Ramesh Damani, that he had met the NBCC management more than he had met his fiancée!

Ramesh Damani Sir's words - "On average, I meet one company a month. When I am researching a company, I initially meet them often. In my mature investments, I don’t meet.

Let me tell you an interesting story. My son Ashok was getting into the business back then. I used to send him out to visit companies. I was buying NBCC in a big way at that time.

He had been to Delhi three to four times, and I used to send him to meet NBCC each time to find out what was going on. Then he got engaged. When he was going to Delhi, I asked him to meet NBCC.

He said that he has met NBCC more than he has met his fiancée! When NBCC got listed, I used to meet them once every two months, but now it’s on autopilot. I haven’t met them in three years now."

A Funny story indeed!

From the Book Masterclass with Super Investors

items.title: RT Ananth Rupanagudi: Lunchtime humour - In a factory, a man was standing on the floor and looking aimlessly. The CEO of that factory came and asked h...
items.description_html:
RT Ananth Rupanagudi
Lunchtime humour - In a factory, a man was standing on the floor and looking aimlessly. The CEO of that factory came and asked his salary.

Man replied - "Rs.5000 sir".

The CEO took out his wallet and gave Rs.15000 and told him "I pay people here to work and not to waste time, This is your 3 months salary. Now get out of here. Never come back".

That guy left. Then CEO asked workers "Who was that guy?"

Workers replied "Pizza delivery Boy, sir".

Moral: Don't overreact in every situation! 😀😛😂

items.title: I just finished reading Sorkin's "1929". It doesn't provide anything new in the way of analysis or explanation of the events of the late 1920s and ear...
items.description_html:
I just finished reading Sorkin's "1929". It doesn't provide anything new in the way of analysis or explanation of the events of the late 1920s and early 1930s, but it is a lot of fun to read, has a huge cast of primary and secondary (and tertiary) characters, and provides a great deal of color on well-known figures like Richard and George Whitney, Charlie Mitchell, William Durant, Carter Glass, and at least a dozen others.

Later today I will be meeting a Chinese student who is going off to Yale in September to study economics and history, and I plan to give him my copy of this book as an ideal way for someone unfamiliar with the history to start learning about one of the most important periods in modern economic history.

items.title: The recent surge in commodity chemical stocks appears largely cyclical, driven by temporary supply side disruptions rather than any meaningful structu...
items.description_html:
The recent surge in commodity chemical stocks appears largely cyclical, driven by temporary supply side disruptions rather than any meaningful structural shift. I remain unconvinced that current geopolitical dynamics are creating a lasting change in the industry’s fundamentals.

There may be isolated, product specific tailwinds, but the broad based recovery across commodity chemical players, from NOCIL to Deepak Nitrite, Ingrevia and DMCC, suggests a cyclical rebound rather than a structural re rating. If this were truly structural, the divergence would likely be more selective and sustained, rather than this synchronised upswing.

That said, it is worth considering whether second order geopolitical effects could gradually reshape supply chains. For now, however, the evidence points more towards cyclical normalisation than a paradigm shift.

items.title: RT Aaron Levie: If you read this and don’t understand why it’s happening it’s an opportunity to reset your understanding of how the real world work...
items.description_html:
RT Aaron Levie
If you read this and don’t understand why it’s happening it’s an opportunity to reset your understanding of how the real world works.

The real world will need a ton of help actually getting agents going in the enterprise. Companies have legacy tech stacks they need to modernize, data in tons of fragmented tools, knowledge that isn’t captured or digitized, and change management needed to actually utilize agents effectively. And they have to do all this while still running their business day-to-day, unlike startups.

This is why there is so much opportunity for companies (software or services) to actually deploy agents in specific domains and workflows. This remains a big opportunity for both existing services providers but also tons of new startups as well. Every new technology wave produces a new era of consulting firms that can deliver on that technology.

It’s also why the FDE model is going to be alive and well for a long time because companies will want to have their vendor actually help drive the change management and implementation for their new workflows.

The people aren’t going away. Far from it.


First Squawk: OPENAI WORKING WITH CONSULTING FIRMS, INCLUDING ACCENTURE, CAPGEMINI AND PWC, TO HELP SELL CODEX TO BUSINESSES- WSJ

Two ways to look at this,

1. Positive: Trump's post doesn't mention nuclear enrichment or anythig else. So will it just be a coordinated opening of both the blockades & we move on ? We will see..

2. Negative: The blockade cannot be endless, but till the time it is on - oil prices stay high. The world & big importers cannot function with oil in the 90s.

Blanchard dismisses the argument that global imbalances create financial risks, bc he says what matters is gross positions, not net. But the fact that financial instability can exist without persistent imbalances doesn’t mean persistent imbalances don't cause it. Just like lung cancer can exist without smoking but that doesn’t mean smoking is at most a marginal contributor to lung cancer. In fact, the causal chain from persistent imbalances to financial instability was mapped out decades ago.

Joan Robinson's beggar-thy-neighbor argument showed that a surplus country can relieve its own demand deficiency by exporting it abroad so that the deficit country is forced to absorb another country's domestic imbalance. When one group of countries runs persistent surpluses and refuses to rebalance by raising domestic demand, some other group of countries has to run the mirror image deficits and absorb the shortfall in global demand.

Keynes explained that the deficit country can respond to these beggar-thy-neighbor policies by either accepting lost output through higher unemployment and deflation, or they can do it by expanding credit and public deficits to sustain demand. Either way, persistent imbalances shift the pressure of adjustment onto balance sheets. That was the entire logic behind his insistence at Bretton Woods that surplus countries had to face pressure to adjust too. If only deficit countries are forced to adjust, you get either economic contraction or rising leverage.

Kindleberger then showed how the capital flows associated with persistent imbalances impact financial markets and build the structures through which instability eventually emerges. Large inflows into a country that doesn't need external savings compress yields, lower risk premia, encourage speculation, and push credit into weaker uses. His work is filled with historical cases where external surpluses and deficits fed lending booms, speculative manias, and bad debt cycles.

Minsky then explained the balance sheet mechanism through which net imbalances create financial instability. If a country accommodates persistent demand leakage through debt growth rather than immediate contraction, the financial system changes character over time. Units that begin in hedge finance move into speculative finance and then Ponzi finance. So persistent external deficits that are accommodated through credit expansion create the kind of environment in which leverage rises, asset prices are bid up, and borrowers become increasingly dependent on rollover conditions staying benign.

Jacques Rueff adds another important piece by pointing out that reserve currency systems allow deficit financing and external adjustment to be postponed far longer than under a harder monetary regime. That delay is destabilizing bc it lets the stock of claims and the underlying distortions grow much larger before it results in a crisis. In a gold system the adjustment is brutal but faster. In a reserve currency system, adjustment can be deferred. While that might look stabilizing for a while, it often means the eventual instability is bigger bc the imbalances have had more time to reshape the credit system and asset markets.
Bernanke acknowledged this structural flaw, stating that the international monetary system "lacks a mechanism, market based or otherwise, to induce needed adjustments by surplus countries, which can result in persistent imbalances." And that “global rebalancing of trade and current accounts, are an essential outcome for long run economic and financial stability.”

The bottom line is that persistent imbalances create financial instability bc a system that forces one set of countries to absorb the counterpart deficits will eventually make them do so through unemployment, debt, or asset bubbles, and that’s exactly how financial fragility builds.


Olivier Blanchard: The argument in the G7 report https://shorturl.at/zZf25 that global imbalances create financial risks is unconvincing.
Suppose there were no imbalances, either in stocks (assets= liabilities) or in flows (zero financial account). The risk that some investors decide to sell would

items.description_html:
RT Richard Wu
The structure of the deal is pretty interesting here. I think what’s happening is:

1. xAI is having trouble training a SOTA coding model (hence cofounder departures), bunch of idle GPUs
2. Cursor doesn’t have capital to blow on a $5B training run to compete with Codex/Claude
3. xAI says to Cursor: use all the GPUs you want at cost and get to a SOTA coding model, as long as we have an option to buy you
4. Cursor also gets a free option: train a model better than Opus and get bought out for $60B, or get $10B that pays for all the GPUs you rented

Win win

items.title: RT Ian Miles Cheong: Marc Andreessen just revealed the Elon Musk philosophy that completely broke his brain: "The best product in the world shouldn't ...
items.description_html:
RT Ian Miles Cheong
Marc Andreessen just revealed the Elon Musk philosophy that completely broke his brain: "The best product in the world shouldn't even need a logo."

We all know Elon is relentless about quality. As Marc puts it: "Do you want the best car in the world or not, right? Like that's Elon's mentality... And it's working very well."

But at a recent event, Elon took this mindset to a completely different level. He dropped a perspective so jarring that Marc initially thought it was a joke.

Elon’s thesis? "You shouldn't even have to have your name on the product. It's just obvious. Everybody knows."

The logic is brutal but simple. If you build the undeniable, undisputed best thing in the world, everybody uses it. And because everybody uses it, you don't need to slap your branding all over it to prove it's yours.

Think about that. We spend endless hours agonizing over marketing, tweaking brand colors, and putting our logos on every square inch of what we build. But the ultimate flex isn't a flashy logo. The ultimate flex is building something so undeniably brilliant that its mere existence is the brand.

items.title: As a country gets richer and simultaneously ages, deaths from communicable diseases and conditions related to childbirth become more preventable and l...
items.description_html:
As a country gets richer and simultaneously ages, deaths from communicable diseases and conditions related to childbirth become more preventable and less common. Consequently, the relative share of deaths from non-communicable diseases such as heart disease and cancer begins to rise.

India is in the midst of this epidemiological transition. Non-communicable diseases account for over half of all deaths in India as of 2022, the latest year for which this data is available.

Among non-communicable diseases, heart disease is the largest contributor to Indian mortality and is now responsible for one in three deaths. From middle age onwards as the relative risk from infectious diseases declines, age and factors including obesity and hypertension put people at greater risk.

Cancer kills over 6% of Indians every year, with a higher contribution to mortality among those in their late 40s and early 50s in particular. 15% of deaths of women aged 30-54 are from cancer.

What are the top causes of death from communicable diseases in India? Read @Rukmini's piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/indias-disease-transition/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Diseases #Health #PublicHealth #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Mr Ramesh Damani - "The way you do investing is - you keep a circle of competence and do your homework, don’t get complacent and lazy, which we tend ...
items.description_html:
Mr Ramesh Damani -

"The way you do investing is - you keep a circle of competence and do your homework, don’t get complacent and lazy, which we tend to be after some time.

If you are generally a smart person, then there will be tons and tons of opportunity. Here is the thing that RK Damani told me. I used to ask him in the 1990s, after he had made tons of money and I hadn’t . I said “you people are lucky that you bought Indian stocks in the 1980s. Aap logon ka paisa bangaya. Mein abhi aaya hoon. Mera Nahi Banega” (“You people have made money. I have just come. I won’t make money.”)

He said, “Ramesh, I am telling you this today. The opportunity in the next 20 years will be far greater than those in the past 20 years. Trust me on that.” He has been proven absolutely correct.

In December 2016, there was a conference in Kolkata. Someone asked him the same question and he answered the same - “The best time to invest in India is today. The opportunity of the last 30 years will be dwarfed by the opportunity of the next 30 years.” I also truly believe that.

From the Book Masterclass with Super Investors

items.title: RT Spharaka Networks: This weekend, a multi-billion dollar tech company was breached. Not through malware. Not through a zero-day. Not through phishin...
items.description_html:
RT Spharaka Networks
This weekend, a multi-billion dollar tech company was breached.

Not through malware. Not through a zero-day. Not through phishing.

Through an AI tool that had OAuth access to an employee's workspace.

The attacker compromised a third-party AI application, inherited its trusted permissions, pivoted into internal systems, and accessed secrets and deployment pipelines, all without writing a single exploit.

This is the new kill chain. And most enterprises are completely unprepared for it.

Every AI agent connected to your email, documents, code repositories, or cloud infrastructure is a privileged insider operating at machine speed. When that agent is compromised, the attacker doesn't need your password. They already have your trust.

The numbers should concern every security leader:

→ 490% increase in AI-related attacks year over year
→ 48% of security professionals rank agentic AI as the #1 attack vector of 2026
→ 86% of organisations have zero visibility into where their AI tools are connected
→ Over half of deployed AI agents operate without any security monitoring

This is the threat model we built Spharaka Sphere™ to defend against, autonomous, AI-native defence that monitors identity chains, detects trust anomalies, and responds at machine speed before human analysts even see an alert.

In a world where attackers use AI agents, defenders need autonomous AI agents too.

The next breach won't come through your firewall. It will come through an integration you already approved.

#CyberSecurity #AIAgents #OAuthSecurity #AttackSurface #SaaSSecurity #IdentitySecurity #SupplyChainRisk #AutonomousDefence #SpharakaNetworks #SpharakaSphere #AuraXP #AgenticAI #CISO #EnterpriseSecurity #InfoSec

https://open.substack.com/pub/spharaka/p/the-trusted-agent-how-ai-copilots?utm_campaign=post-expanded-share&utm_medium=web

items.title: Finally some good progress on Market Coupling - Round Robin system is no more - Grid India takes Operator status to match bids as a neutral party - Gr...
items.description_html:
Finally some good progress on Market Coupling

- Round Robin system is no more
- Grid India takes Operator status to match bids as a neutral party
- Grid India has 6 months to come up with the Market coupling algorithm similar to Europe's Day Ahead Market
- Post 6 months, then after everyone's approval they have to build the systems, the infrastructure, cybersecurity protection etc etc

Looks like this should take 12 to 18months to see some value

This is assuming IEX does not put new cases on the order to stall

Now contrary to popular belief - Market Coupling like the EU is actually good

- EU trading Market boomed post market Coupling
- The industry leader EPEX Spot who was a market leader before coupling still has most of the volume
- EU has only 2 players taking all the market share with Nord Pool being 2nd

IEX
- Yes market coupling without round Robin is good, but market coupling itself will take some market share
- At best market share should dip from 99% to 75%.
- At best post market coupling they would stall revenues for 1 year
- Post market coupling the regulator will boost Power trading a lot
- Volume will rise
- News instruments like CfD coming to power exchanges

Verdict
Short term IEX will be volatile
For long term investors the game will start post Market Coupling

Today Indias largest Power exchange is at close to a billion dollars.

Disc - Invested in IEX at Lower Levels. Not a buy or sell reco

items.title: RT Sridhar Vembu: I am happy to see the YCombinator wave arriving in India. Our co-founder Tony and I were in silicon valley during the original YC wa...
items.description_html:
RT Sridhar Vembu
I am happy to see the YCombinator wave arriving in India. Our co-founder Tony and I were in silicon valley during the original YC wave of companies. We learned a lot from the YC companies. We also made the conscious choice to pursue a different course.

In the ancient Bharatiya tradition of philosophical debate, I am going to offer this.

I will start with things I agree with YC on.

1. The biggest lesson anyone can learn from YC: small passionate teams can do magic. This was true way before AI coding arrived and will always be true.

2. YC is absolutely right to not over-emphasize "innovation". Doing a similar product as the bigger guys, faster and cheaper, is often the best course. Google did not invent search. OpenAI did not invent the LLM. Anthropic did not invent agentic coding.

3. YC companies tend to geographically cluster, and that can lead to subtle peer pressure and group-think. So the "laggard" founders who are not growing like a weed every week start to feel left out and eventually the result is "founder depression". And YC has counselors.

By the standards of 2007 silicon valley or even 2014 silicon valley, we were thought to be losers. Just keep that in mind.

4. Many deep tech problems like building a better, cheaper MRI machine or advanced semiconductor equipment require long focus and patient execution and lots of capital. These are endurance tests, not weekly sprints.

5. YC too often optimizes companies for "exit". That philosophy was built for and requires prolonged bubbles, which American policy has delivered, at the price of nearly wrecking the country (note the extreme inequality and political division). If you love India, you should not wish for similar bubbles.

6. YC model worked in silicon valley. One of the reasons it worked was that silicon valley could get any talent from anywhere in the world, notably from India, easily. That era may have ended or at least on pause right now.

Bengaluru has tried the same thing but with "any talent from anywhere in India" and we have not yet created huge companies. India needs its Huawei and Xiaomi and BYD and these companies are Chinese to the core, built by patriotic Chinese. Indian talent, staying in India, rooted in India, is going to have to build companies like them. Enough said.


Jared Friedman: What I told 2,000 future founders in Bengaluru today:

1/ We believe we are at the start of a second wave of Indian companies that will build world-class AI native products for the global market. Emergent and Giga are the model of the future.

2/ Just because a space seems

items.title: Raamdeo Agrawal Sir on how his investing journey started – "During my CA course in Mumbai, I somehow got hooked onto the stock markets. I got to lear...
items.description_html:
Raamdeo Agrawal Sir on how his investing journey started –

"During my CA course in Mumbai, I somehow got hooked onto the stock markets. I got to learn about companies like Century Textiles, where I was doing the audits. Meanwhile, in 1980, my brother got into the merchant navy. He was making good money - around Rs.30,000-40,000 a month. He had saved about Rs. 4-5 lakh.

I suggested he invest that money in the stock market. I told him that I would help him invest. So that’s how I started my journey of investing in the stock markets in 1980, with my brother’s money. We went to the brokers for information, started reading accountancy books (which were not readily available), and international magazines like Fortune, which carried stock-oriented articles.

The good thing was that I was a habitual reader right from my childhood. As far as I can remember, I was a voracious reader. I used to go to the library almost every day and borrow whichever books I could lay my hands on, though it was mostly fiction.

After I completed my CA, I wondered what to do in life. The only thing that interested me was the stock market. I tried to practice as a CA for a year but realised pretty early that it was not my cup of tea. Then I decided to get into equity research. I

saw an advertisement for an equity research job in a company called ‘Aditi Investments’. It was like a God-sent offer for me. Can you believe that I was the only person who applied for that job? I got the job.

By that time, I had collected and read balance sheets for about 250 companies. My weekend job and passion were to collect balance sheets. I was so fascinated by everything. In the early 1980s, there were no databases or the internet. If I met somebody and got to know that his father invests in the stock market, I would make it a point to go to his house, make friends with his father, and gather all the balance sheets!

So, by 1985, I had read and internalised all the balance sheets by page. I had analysed them by capacity, location, utilization, cost structure, profit structure, etc. I became a moving encyclopaedia on that many stocks. Simultaneously, I was investing Rs. 5-6 lakh for my brother. I used to think I was a smart person. I did two years of formal equity research. I wrote a book, Corporate Numbers Game, in 1986

From the Book Masterclass with Super Investors

What's unfolding at Pepsico(with Siemens & NVIDIA) is going to set the narrative for the foreseeable future. Investory management, product mix, precise assortment planning, procurement & sales, pricing mix, all are set to transform. And, this play has many proxy plays that few are willing to look at.

items.description_html:
Strait of Hormuz is open during ceasefire, Iran says.
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said the Strait of Hormuz was open following a ceasefire accord agreed in Lebanon, while US President Donald Trump said he believed a deal to end the Iran war would come "soon," although the timing remains unclear.
Araghchi said in a post on X the Strait was open for all commercial vessels for the remainder of the U.S.-brokered 10-day truce between Israeli forces and Iran-backed Hezbollah agreed between Israel and Lebanon.
He said the passage of ships would need to be along the route that Iran's Ports and Maritime Organisation had announced.
@Reuters

items.title: Did you know why Nuclear energy is making a comeback? One kilogram of enriched uranium (4% U-235) produces roughly 160,000 kWh of electricity. For com...
items.description_html:
Did you know why Nuclear energy is making a comeback?

One kilogram of enriched uranium (4% U-235) produces roughly 160,000 kWh of electricity. For comparison:

1 kg of coal → 3 kWh
1 kg of natural gas → 15 kWh
1 kg of diesel → 12 kWh

This is not a marginal improvement. It is a 50,000x advantage over coal by mass. It's why one standard 700 MWe PHWR in India replaces a 2,800 MW coal plant equivalent on annual energy.

items.title: While most Indian households now own a mobile phone, computer ownership in India has grown slowly. Over the last two decades, the share of households ...
items.description_html:
While most Indian households now own a mobile phone, computer ownership in India has grown slowly. Over the last two decades, the share of households that own a computer has increased from just over 1% to just over 9%. In most developed countries, by contrast, computer ownership exceeds 75%.

Among the 30 million households in India that own a computer, 90% have a laptop, while 20% own a desktop. A little under 10% of computer-owning households report having both a laptop and a desktop.

India has also made limited progress in the ability to use computers over the last decade. A little over one in five adults, or 200 million people, were able to operate a computer as of 2025. This share is roughly twice the share of households that own a computer, suggesting that many people may have learned to use a computer, or use one in college or at work, without owning one themselves.

While the proportion of rural adults who can use a computer has increased, it still stands at just about one in six. Among urban adults, the figure is much higher with over one in three adults, but it has grown only marginally over the last decade.

How does the ability to use computers vary by age group and gender in India? Read @akwaghmare’s piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/computers/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Computer #Technology #Laptop #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Paytm owner One97 Communications has now become an Indian-owned and controlled company (IOCC). Until now, IOCC status has been significant for online ...
items.description_html:
Paytm owner One97 Communications has now become an Indian-owned and controlled company (IOCC).

Until now, IOCC status has been significant for online retailers, as it allows them to hold inventory.

Eternal-owned Blinkit became one through a QIP, Swiggy is close to getting there, and Nykaa has always had this advantage.

Paytm is among the first major listed fintechs to get IOCC status.

There may not be immediate, direct business benefits, but this is important given the scrutiny Paytm has faced over ownership by China’s Alibaba and Ant Financial, which together held 38% before the IPO.

After the IPO in 2021, foreign investors held 81%, which has now come down to just below 50%.

items.title: #LaurusLabs, at today’s not-so-cheap valuations and hovering near its all-time highs, is not a HOLD if your view is just 3 months. But stretch that v...
items.description_html:
#LaurusLabs, at today’s not-so-cheap valuations and hovering near its all-time highs, is not a HOLD if your view is just 3 months. But stretch that view to 3 years, and it still might be.

This gap comes down to time. As Howard Marks and Nassim Taleb remind us, risk and value shift with the clock. What feels expensive and fragile in the short run can turn reasonable once time allows the business to prove itself.

In 'Fooled by Randomness', Taleb tells a story about a retired dentist who is a skilled investor. The dentist expects to earn about 15% more than safe government bonds each year. But he checks his portfolio many times a day. This habit causes stress because, in the short term, prices move almost randomly. Taleb calls this “noise”, which simply means meaningless fluctuations that do not reflect real value.

Over very short periods, like minutes or days, the chance of seeing a gain is barely above 50%. This means that over three months, price movements are driven mostly by luck and maybe Q4 FY26 numbers or, rather, the interpretation of those. If a stock is already “not-so-cheap”, its price reflects a lot of optimism. There is little room for disappointment. Even a small negative surprise can push the price down. It's always albout the rate of change relative to the asking run-rate (valuations)

In 'Most Important Thing', Marks explains that risk often comes from paying too high a price. When you buy an expensive stock, you have almost no “margin for error”. This simply means you have little room to be wrong and still be okay. In the short term, bad luck or shifting sentiment can cause sharp falls. He also points out that being early can feel the same as being wrong, especially over short periods.

Taleb offers another example in 'Fooled by Randomness'. He describes the idea of building a ski resort in Miami. Imagine a rare blizzard hits, and the resort makes a huge profit in its first year. That success does not make it a good decision. It was simply luck. In the same way, making money on an expensive stock over 3 months may just mean you were lucky, not right.

Things look different over longer periods. As time passes, real business performance begins to matter more than daily price swings. Taleb notes that over a year, the odds of seeing gains improve greatly. Over 3 years, the company’s actual progress has a much better chance to show through the noise. What the business earns and builds starts to matter more than short-term sentiment.

Back in 2023, I was one of the few bullish voices on Laurus. Talk is cheap, so I backed that view with my own money. Sell ratings were common at the time, and many were anchored to targets around 250, which weighed heavily on sentiment.

Marks explains that while prices can drift in the short run, terminal value pulls them back over time. Terminal value is simply what a business is truly worth based on what it can earn and build.

For Laurus Labs, the CDMO business is that anchor. Over the next five to seven years, this segment alone could cross 10,000 Cr. in annual sales. Then, post-2033, the precision fermentation and bio-engine should be all guns blazing.

If a company grows well, it can “grow into” a high MCap. This is why a stock that looks expensive today may still work out over a few years. Over 3 years, a strong business may justify a high starting price. Over 3 months, you are mostly exposed to chance.

Taleb compares short-term risk to a version of Russian roulette with many chambers. You might avoid losses several times, but the risk is always there. Without a margin of safety, a sudden drop can happen at any time. This is why short-term investing in expensive stocks can feel unpredictable and fragile.

In the end, time changes the nature of the bet. Over 3 months, randomness dominates, and a high price leaves little protection. Over 3 years, business performance has time to come through, and value can catch up with price. The same stock can look risky or reasonable depending on how long you plan to hold it.

Laurus Labs is our core family holding, and none of this is an investment recommendation.

items.title: Organised manufacturing accounts for the majority of economic value added through manufacturing and employed 18.5 million people in over 200,000 facto...
items.description_html:
Organised manufacturing accounts for the majority of economic value added through manufacturing and employed 18.5 million people in over 200,000 factories in 2024.

The manufacture of food products employs the most Indian factory workers, accounting for over two million jobs. The manufacture of textiles and wearing apparel are other key sources of factory jobs, collectively employing over three million people.

The manufacture of food products is widely dispersed throughout the country, with Maharashtra being the leading employer. Tamil Nadu followed by Gujarat are India's textile manufacturing hubs. Tamil Nadu is India's apparel manufacturing hub, with more than one in three apparel factory workers in the state alone.

The manufacture of basic metals is largely confined to the mineral-rich states of Odisha, Chhattisgarh and West Bengal, as well as Maharashtra and Gujarat.

How much economic value does organised manufacturing create across different regions in India? Read Ishan Deodhar and @akwaghmare's piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/manufacturing-hubs/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Manufacturing #Industry #Economy #India #DataForIndia

items.title: 🚨 Superhealth and Even Healthcare are among a group of startups testing hospital models built on smaller facilities, lower upfront investment, and ...
items.description_html:
🚨 Superhealth and Even Healthcare are among a group of startups testing hospital models built on smaller facilities, lower upfront investment, and standardised pricing.

Their thesis isn’t that conventional hospitals or large chains can be replaced, but that short-stay and elective care can be delivered more efficiently through a leaner model.

Instead of replicating the full-service, capital-intensive format of multi-speciality hospitals, they are building 50–70 bed facilities focused on procedures with an average length of stay (ALOS) of under two nights.

This narrower clinical scope, they believe, enables faster patient turnover and keeps per-bed capital costs in check.

In the past, these efforts haven't worked, but these startups are looking to set a new precedent.

https://www.thearcweb.com/article/superhealth-even-healthcare-hospitals-medical-services-funding-jZaCa17UwFMDsJl8

items.title: The rise of mobile phones powered access to the internet in India. In the ten years from 2014 to 2024, India added 30 million wired internet connectio...
items.description_html:
The rise of mobile phones powered access to the internet in India. In the ten years from 2014 to 2024, India added 30 million wired internet connections. In the same time, India added nearly 900 million mobile broadband connections.

Household surveys point to a similar expansion of household-level internet access; only 2% of homes in India had internet access in 2009, but once mobile broadband entered the space, the pace of access accelerated. By 2023, this had increased to 76% of households.

Alongside household access, the usage of the internet has grown too. As of 2023, nearly six in ten Indians had used the internet at least once in the last three months, according to a nationally representative survey.

Internet usage is higher among more urban, better educated and richer Indians, and more among men than women. Internet usage is also rising steadily - the share of Indian teenagers using the internet is double that of Indians above the age of 60 using it.

How does internet access in India look across different income and education levels? Read @akwaghmare’s analysis to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/comm-tech/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Mobile #Phone #Internet #India #DataForIndia

items.title: RT Nithin Kamath: I recently had dinner with Dr Devi Shetty, the founder of Narayana Hospitals. For those who don't know him, he's the guy who figured...
items.description_html:
RT Nithin Kamath
I recently had dinner with Dr Devi Shetty, the founder of Narayana Hospitals. For those who don't know him, he's the guy who figured out how to do open heart surgery for a few hundred dollars when the same procedure costs a bomb in the US. Narayana has 18,000 beds across India, and if you ask most middle-class people in Bangalore about it, they'll speak highly of it.

There was one thing I kept thinking about over and over again after meeting him.

Narayana's market cap is around ₹38,000 crore. Now compare that to pretty much any half-decent financial services business in India, and it'll be valued more than that, including Zerodha. A brokerage, worth more than a hospital chain, that has probably saved hundreds of thousands of lives.

I get the arguments. If you're a fund manager/analyst, you can immediately explain it away using margins, capex, asset-light vs asset-heavy, and all that, and I'm not saying the market is wrong.

But it's still a strange world we've built, where the businesses closest to money get valued the highest, and the ones doing the hard and essential things get priced like boring utilities. A hospital carries physical infrastructure, enormous liability, thin margins and the actual weight of keeping people alive. And somehow that's worth less than a platform for buying and selling stocks.

I don't have a clean take on this. All of this just felt odd.

Ps: Nothing here is investment advice. For that, go to @zerodhavarsity
